coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer encapsulated with quantum dots for detection of crocin as an alternative approach for quality estimation of saffron

coredata.dc:creator: Mir, Tahir ul Gani
Shukla, Saurabh
Singh, Jaskaran
Gacem, Amel
Ballal, Suhas
Yadav, Krishna Kumar
Mansour, Lamjed
Kumar, Abhinav
Dar, Mudasir A.
Wani, Atif Khurshid
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (MIPs) are widely used for their selective recognition capabilities, synthesized through interactions between template molecules, functional monomers, crosslinkers, and solvents. In this study, MIPs encapsulated with Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized for detection of crocin, a bioactive carotenoid responsible for saffron’s vivid colour. CQDs were synthesized via thermal pyrolysis and characterized using TEM, FTIR, XRD, XPS, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The MIP-CQDs nanocomposite was prepared by integrating CQDs with functional monomers and crosslinkers, followed by template removal. Comparative non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) were also synthesized. The nanocomposites exhibited excellent optical and morphological properties, with fluorescence quenching used to evaluate crocin interaction. The MIP-CQDs displayed high sensitivity and selectivity for crocin in a linear range of 2–175 μM, with a detection limit of 2.1 μM. Optimal conditions included pH 7.0 and a 5-minute incubation time. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between crocin and MIP-CQDs. Notably, MIP-CQDs showed strong resistance to interference from structurally similar compounds and co-existing ions, ensuring reliable performance in complex matrices. The study underscores the significance of tailored nanocomposite designs for advancing molecular sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly Imprinted Polymers
Carbon Quantum Dots
Crocin
Saffron
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent probe for detection of crocin
Collection and preparation of saffron samples
Characterization of CQDs, MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs
Interaction between crocin and MIP sensor
Analysis of crocin in saffron sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b), XPS (c) and XRD (d) of CQDs.
Size distribution (a), zeta potential (b) of CQDs.
SEM image of MIP-CQDs (a) and NIP-CQDs (b), FTIR spectra of MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs (c), XRD of MIP-CQDs (b) and XRD of NIP-CQDs (e).
Interaction of different concentrations of crocin with MIP-CQD sensor (a), Linear range of MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs towards crocin (b).

The effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity of MIP-CQDS (a), fluorescent responses of MIP-CQDs in the presence of crocin at different intervals of time (b), and selectivity of NCQDS towards crocin in the presence of interfering substances (c).
Comparison of Analytical Methods for Crocin Detection in Saffron.
Quantitative measurement of crocin in saffron using MIPs-NCQDS.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer encapsulated with quantum dots for detection of crocin as an alternative approach for quality estimation of saffron

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (MIPs) are widely used for their selective recognition capabilities, synthesized through interactions between template molecules, functional monomers, crosslinkers, and solvents. In this study, MIPs encapsulated with Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized for detection of crocin, a bioactive carotenoid responsible for saffron’s vivid colour. CQDs were synthesized via thermal pyrolysis and characterized using TEM, FTIR, XRD, XPS, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The MIP-CQDs nanocomposite was prepared by integrating CQDs with functional monomers and crosslinkers, followed by template removal. Comparative non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) were also synthesized. The nanocomposites exhibited excellent optical and morphological properties, with fluorescence quenching used to evaluate crocin interaction. The MIP-CQDs displayed high sensitivity and selectivity for crocin in a linear range of 2–175 μM, with a detection limit of 2.1 μM. Optimal conditions included pH 7.0 and a 5-minute incubation time. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between crocin and MIP-CQDs. Notably, MIP-CQDs showed strong resistance to interference from structurally similar compounds and co-existing ions, ensuring reliable performance in complex matrices. The study underscores the significance of tailored nanocomposite designs for advancing molecular sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Molecularly Imprinted Polymer (MIP) technique revolves around creating specific binding sites that closely resemble those of the template molecule. It comprisesa target sample, functional monomers, and a cross-linker for the polymerization process. The interaction between the template and the monomer is crucial for the formation of a MIP [1–3]. The interaction of the active monomer with the crosslinker and solvent is also essential during the bond interaction [4,5]. The selectivity of MIPs depends on how the crosslinker interacts with the targetmolecule. The porogenic solvent is crucial in the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). Porogenic solvents often interact with distinct templates, functional monomers, and crosslinkers [4]. The kind and concentration of porogenic solvents affect the extent of noncovalent interactions and the shape of the polymer, thus influencing MIP production [6]. The sample molecule, monomer, initiator, and crosslinker must all be soluble in porogenic Solvent. Additionally, to minimize the barriers that arise during the intricate organization of the monomer and imprint molecule, the solvent should generate large pores. Thus, the porogenic solvent should possess a medium polarity. Saffron is one of the most expensive spices and is often called red gold. Due to its high demand and low production, it is prone to adulteration. The quality of saffron is based on the concentration of its metabolites [7,8]. Saffron is abundant in volatile, nonvolatile, and aromatic compounds. These compounds consist of hydrophilic and hydrophilic proteins, carbohydrates, minerals, vitamins, polysaccharides, pigments, mucilage, saponins, alkaloids, crocins, safranal, picrocrocin, crocetin, and other compounds in trace amounts [9]. Crocetin and its related glucosidic derivatives are the primary metabolitespresent in the reddish-coloured stigmatic lobes of saffron [10]. The primary metaboliteof saffron that contributes to qualityare crocin, picrocrocin and safranal. Picrocrocin upon hydrolysis results in the formationof safranal and glucose [11]. Crocins are abundant in the Crocus genus. The chemical formula of the substance is C44H70O28, and its molecular weight is 976.96 g/mol. Crocin is a water-soluble gentiobiose compound of carotenoids with cis and trans diesters that is frequently used as a colouring agent in a variety of foods and medications [12,13]. Crocin, which is composed of trans-crocetin di-D gentiobiosyl, is responsible for the orange-yellow hue colour of the saffron [11,12]. Crocins may be either the monoglycosyl polyene or di-glycosyl polyene ester of crocetin. Crocin is an ideal coloring agent for a diverse array of foods and cuisines due to the presence of esterified gentiobioses. The quality of saffron is mainly based on the concentration of crocin, Simply, the Higher the concentration of Crocin, the higher the quality of saffron [7,14–16]. The ISO (ISO 3632) has classified saffron into different grades (Grades I, II, III, IV) [17]. The grading of saffron as per ISO is mainly based on the concentration of crocin in saffron. Therefore, developing a cost-effective and rapid method for detecting the concentration of crocin in saffron is essential. In this study, MIP-QDs were synthesized for quantifying the crocin concentration in saffron. Crocin, a bioactive compound responsible for the vivid colour of saffron, necessitates accurate detection to evaluate saffron quality and prevent adulteration. To accomplish this, a nanoscale probe using a combination of MIPs and CQDs was synthesized.

To synthesize CQDs, a refined technique called thermal pyrolysis, as described by Dong et al., (2012), was utilized with some modifications [18]. In this process, an optimized amount of 4.0 g of citric acid powder dissolved in distilled water was heated at 180 °C for 10 min in a microwave oven until the solution changed to a yellow hue, indicating the formation of CQDs [18,19]. The characterization of the CQDs involved several techniques, including Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence spectroscopy (FS), and Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR). It is pertinent to say that the physicochemical properties of the synthesized CQDs, such as particle size, fluorescence behaviour, and surface functionality, are influenced by the synthesis parameters. Therefore, factors such as pyrolysis temperature, reaction time, and precursor concentration play an important role in determining the consistency as well as the quality of the synthesized CQDs. In this study, synthesis conditions were optimized—specifically, pyrolysis was carried out at 180 °C for 10 min—to ensure reproducibility and achieve desirable optical properties. Any variation in these parameters could potentially lead to changes in emission wavelength, quantum yield, or particle stability, which may in turn affect the performance of the MIP-CQDs in sensing applications [20–25]. Therefore, strict control over synthesis conditions was maintained throughout the preparation process.

Next, the CQDs were subjected to further modification. Initially, 5 ml of APTES was added to the CQDs, and then 0.05 g of gentiobiose was dissolved in 70 % methanol (v/v). Subsequently, optimized amounts of TEOS and a catalyst solution (100 μL), prepared using 0.036 g NH4F, 9.2 ml NH4OH, and 20 ml water, were added to the mixture. The resulting blend was stirred for 60 min at 35 °C to create the MIP-CQDs. The nanocomposite was subjected to template removal through centrifugation and washing with a mixture of deionized water and methanol. Successful removal of the template was confirmed using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The resulting material the MIP-CQD nanocomposite, was stored for future use. To synthesize the CQDs-NIP, the same procedure was followed, but in the absence of the gentiobiose template.

The morphology of CQDs was studied through TEM, while the zeta potential and particle analyzer (Malvern) were used to determine the surface charge and average diameter. Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) was carried out using a Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 5/5 μM slit. FTIR spectra, spanning 400–4000 cm − 1, were captured using an FTIR spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU AIM-8800). To investigate the crystalline structure of the compound, X-ray Diffraction (BRUKER D8 ADVANCE) was utilized. The absorption spectra were captured using a UV–Vis double beam spectrophotometer (UV-1900I-SHIMADZU). SEM was employed to investigate the morphology of MIP and NIP. XPS analysis was conducted to determine the elemental composition of CQDs. Ultra-pure DI water was utilized in all experiments. Gentiobiose was used as the template for the synthesis of MIP as crocin is a large molecule, it is not possible to use Crocin itself as a template. Structurally, Crocin is made up of two Gentiobiose molecules therefore, gentiobiose was used as a template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent probe for detection of crocin
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the fluorescent probe, dried MIP-CQDs powder (0.5 g) was sonicated for 03 h in a 200 ml-volumetric flask containing buffer (BR), then cooled at room temperature, and 1 ml of the solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette, and it was then mixed with 3 ml of a BR buffer solution. The varying concentration of crocin 2 μm to 200 μm was prepared in 70 % ethanol and 100 μL added to the fluorescent quartz and the fluorescence spectrum was measured for each concertation. The same procedure was applied for NIP-CQDs for comparative analysis. The molecular recognition mechanism of the synthesized nanocomposite is based on the formation of specific binding sites in the MIP composite that are complementary to the template molecule (gentiobiose). After template removal, these sites retain the memory of the template’s spatial arrangement, allowing the rebinding of crocin which is structurally similar to gentiobiose as discussed above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and preparation of saffron samples
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of saffron were procured from local markets situated in Pampore, Kashmir, India. Pampore, a district in Kashmir, has gained recognition for its exceptional saffron cultivation. To ensure the presence of any impurities, the collected samples underwent analysis employing the ISO-3632 technique. These samples were then finely ground to a powder consistency with the assistance of a mortar and pestle. Approximately 100 mg of the ground sample was placed into a screw-capped test tube, followed by the addition of 100 ml of water. The resulting mixture underwent sonication for 30 min, and subsequent centrifugation for around 30 min at a speed of 10000 rpm. The supernatant obtained after centrifugation was then filtered and properly stored at a temperature of 4 °C [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer were utilized to examine the optical characteristics of CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). In the UV region of the spectrum, the CQDs exhibited absorption peaks, which can be mainly ascribed to the n-p and p-p* transitions triggered by the molecular bonds of CO and CC. Fig. 1 (a) also depicts the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs, which demonstrate their excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behaviour. The as-synthesized water-soluble CQDs demonstrated excellent fluorescent properties and excitation wavelength-dependent emission.

In Fig. 1(b), the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs is presented. The wide absorption band observed at 3496.8 cm1 was identified as resulting from the abundance of hydroxyl group residues (–OH) present on the surface of the CQDs. The presence of peaks at 2415.7 cm1 was attributed to aliphatic CH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1644.1 cm1 and 1210 cm1 corresponded to the CC bonds and epoxide groups (COC) bond present in the CQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the surface chemical composition and element status of CQDs. The XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 1(c) displays two peaks at 532.3 eV and 286 eV, corresponding to O1s, and C1s, respectively. This indicates that CQDs are predominantly composed of carbon and oxygen.

Fig. 1(d) shows the XRD pattern of CQDs, which reveals a broad diffraction peak at 18.06° and a weak peak at 25.4°. These peaks suggest that CQDs contain both an amorphous carbon phase and partially graphitized carbon. This is due to the abundance of functional groups present and the graphitization of carbon atoms, respectively. These findings confirm the existence of a graphite structure in the carbon core of CQDs. Taken together, these results indicate that small carbon dots were produced through the hydrothermal synthesis of microcrystalline cellulose with ethylenediamine and that their carbon core structures are similar to those of graphite.

The particle size of the CQDs was determined by the Zeta size analyser and the average size of CQDs was found to be 5.4 μM [Fig. 2 (a)] and the zeta potential of synthesized CQDs was found to be −04.33 [Fig. 2 (b)]. Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b) depict the SEM of MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs respectively. The morphology and the diameter of the MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs were found almost similar.

The FT-IR spectra of MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs, illustrated in Fig. 3(c), were acquired to ascertain the effective chemical changes in each synthesis method. The spectra exhibited a pronounced and extensive absorption peak at 3394 cm-1, signifying stretching vibrations of the − OH group. The peak at 2990 cm−1 corresponded to CH stretching of aromatic hydrocarbons (sp2 carbon), whilst the bands at 1730 and 1762 cm−1 were attributed to C − O and C=H stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1072 cm−1 was attributed to the CO bond. The MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs FTIR spectra showed similar absorption peaks, suggesting effective synthesis of the polymer and removal of the template molecule.

The XRD profile in Fig. 3(d) and 3(e) showed the nature of the MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs. The XRD patterns of the MIPs and NIPs were similar, with a broad hump around 2θ = 21.4° degree corresponding to the plane C(002), indicating their comparative chemical compositions and amorphous nature of CQDs. The similarity of the XRD patterns for both MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs implied comparable chemical compositions in terms of the carbon quantum dots present within their respective structures. The lack of distinct differences in the XRD patterns suggested that the core structural composition of the carbon quantum dots was consistent between the two types of samples, regardless of whether they were embedded within a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) or a non-imprinted polymer (NIP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between crocin and MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the newly developed nanosensor was assessed under optimal conditions. Calibration series of crocin at different concentrations were added to MIP-CQDs and NIP-CQDs, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded (Fig. 4 a). The as-synthesized CQDs-MIP exhibited excellent response within the linear range of 02.0––175.0 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.1 μM (Fig. 4a). The determination coefficient (R2) values for CQDs-MIP and CQDs-NIP were 0.995 and 0.991, respectively. The slope of the calibration curve for CQDs-MIP was higher than that of CQDs-NIP, indicating that CQDs-MIP was more sensitive (Fig. 4b). The proposed optical nanosensor was highly sensitive with a low LOD and thus efficient enough for trace analysis of crocin. The fluorescence spectra measurements for each concentration of crocin were obtained five times, giving a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 2.9 %. The fluorescence quenching process of MIP-CQDs relies on the interaction between the crocin molecule and functional cavities. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of crocin and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs-MIP were examined. The full overlap between them suggests that the fluorescence quenching process is a form of energy transfer, calledFörster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET).

The MIP-CQDs nanocomposite's fluorescence intensity was examined across a pH range of 5.0 to 9.0 [Fig. 5 (a)]. Briefly, 0.10 g of MIP-CQDs was dissolved in 100 ml of buffer (BR) for 02 h, and the concentration of Crocin was kept constant for all measurements. The nanosensor's response intensity decreased in both basic (pH greater than 08.0) and acidic (pH less than 07.0) environments. The reduction in intensity in basic environments was attributed to a decrease in interactions between empty sites and crocin molecules, while the decrease in acidic environments was due to hydrogen bonding disturbance. In pH values greater than 7, surface defects resulting from the ionization of the silica layer likely caused the amount of quenching to decrease. Therefore, for all the experiments, a neutral pH of 07.0 was chosen as the optimal.

The time required for the MIP-CQDs nanocomposite to effectively adsorb crocin was determined by measuring the decrease in fluorescence intensity [Fig. 5(b)]. The optimal conditions for this study were a pH of 7.0, a crocin concentration of 10 μM, and a temperature of 25 ± 1 °C. The incubation time varied between 01 and 10 min. The highest amount of quenching was observed after 05 min, and this time was selected as the optimal incubation time for analyzing crocin.

The effectiveness of the designed nanosensor in measuring crocin was evaluated by testing its selectivity towards other compounds such as safranal and picrocrocin (as shown in Fig. 5(c). The synthesis of CQDs-MIP created specific binding sites on the imprinted polymer that were similar in size, shape, and spatial arrangement to the crocin molecule, making it a better fit for occupying binding sites and causing a more significant decrease in fluorescence intensity than its analogues. The NIP-CQDs did not show significant quenching in fluorescence intensity for crocin and similar compounds, indicating that crocin did not bind well to NIP-CQDs. The impact of co-existing ions on the nanosensor's performance was also examined by measuring crocin in the presence of various ions (such as Fructose, Glucose, Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, NaCl, KCl Na+, and Zn2 + ) (as shown in Fig. 5(c)). The results indicated that the presence of foreign ions in the solution did not significantly affect the fluorescence quenching of the fluorescence probe during the detection of crocin.

A neutral pH of 7.0 was identified as the optimal condition for detection purposes and an incubation time of 5 min was found to be most effective for the adsorption of crocin onto the MIP-CQDs nanocomposite. Moreover, the nanosensor's performance remained unaffected by the presence of various co-existing ions commonly found in complex solutions. This robustness ensures accurate crocin detection even in the presence of potential interfering species. Therefore, the synthesized MIP-CQDs nanocomposite holds promise as a sensitive and specific nanosensor for crocin detection. The combination of optimal pH and incubation time, selectivity for crocin, and resistance to interference from co-existing ions highlights its potential application in real-world scenarios where precise detection of crocin is crucial. This research underscores the significance of nanocomposite design and its potential contribution to the field of molecular sensing and detection.

Table 1 presents a comparison of several methods of detection of crocin in saffron. The proposed MIP-based technique provides an excellent alternative approach for the detection of crocin and a more effective linear range compared to previous studies. This approach may be used to analyse crocin concentration in saffron samples for quality control. This approach provides excellent selectivity in comparison to the other instrumental techniques as outlined in Table 1, and it eliminates the need for an expert to operate such sophisticated instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of crocin in saffron sample
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the crocin concentration in real samples of saffron from three different geographical locations, fluorescence spectra were obtained. The recovery percentage of the samples was found to be 98.6 to 99.02 % (Table 2 ). Therefore, it is evident that the NIP-QDS can be used as an ideal candidate for the detection of crocin in saffron samples.

MIP-CQDs were synthesized to determine the crocin concentration in saffron. Crocin is a bioactive compound responsible for the vibrant color and medicinal properties of saffron, making its accurate detection essential for assessing saffron quality and preventing adulteration. To achieve this, we developed a nanoprobe using MIPs and Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). The first step of the objective involved the meticulous synthesis and characterization of CQDs. Next Quantum dots were used in MIPs to synthesize MIPs-CQDs. Gentiobiose, structurally similar to Crocin, served as a suitable template for MIP-CQDs synthesis. Various instruments were used for the characterization of the Qds and MIPs. Different concentrations of Crocin were prepared in ethanol, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded to establish a calibration series. The nanosensor exhibited impressive sensitivity, showing a linear response in the concentration range from 02.0 to 175.0 μM, with a remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 2.1 μM. The study also delved into optimizing various parameters (ph. Incubation time and interfering substances) to enhance the nanosensor's performance. To validate the nano sensor’s practical applicability, saffron samples from different geographical locations were analyzed. The nanosensor accurately measured Crocin concentrations in the samples, and the results were compared to those obtained from the standard High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) method, showing excellent agreement. Therefore, this study presents a novel and sophisticated nanosensor based on MIP-CQDs for the sensitive and specific detection of Crocin in saffron. The successful synthesis and optimization of the nanosensor, along with its accuracy in saffron sample analysis, offer immense potential for applications in saffron quality control and assurance of authenticity. This research not only advances the field of food analysis but also opens doors for further exploration and potential commercialization of the nanosensor technology for various other applications in different industries.

Tahir ul Gani Mir: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Saurabh Shukla: Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jaskaran Singh: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Amel Gacem: Visualization, Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Suhas Ballal: Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Krishna Kumar Yadav: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lamjed Mansour: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Abhinav Kumar: Software, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mudasir Ahmad Dar: Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Atif Khurshid Wani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP 2025R75), King Saudi University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
